coredata.dc:title: Electronic and magnetic properties of stacked graphene quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Tiutiunnyk, A.
Laroze, D.
Correa, J.D.
Mora-Ramos, M.E.
coredata.dc:description: The electronic properties of vertically coupled stacked graphene quantum dots (GQDs) of triangular shape are investigated using density functional theory, including the influence of applied electric field. Both bilayer and trilayer configurations with different sizes are considered, and quantum dot edges are assumed to be passivated with hydrogen atoms. The electric field has, indeed, an effect on the relative positions of atoms in the layers and also on the inter-layer distances, although it is very slight, just reaching up to half an Angstrom. Electronic states in the dots are not largely affected by the electric field in the case of AA and AAA stacks, but significant variations are induced by it in the case of AB and ABA structures, mainly in the states with energies in the vicinity if the Fermi level. Magnetic features are reported for zigzag structures via the calculation of total spin moment. No magnetic response associated with spin is present on the case of AA bilayer GQDs, whereas zigzag-edged trilayer ABA and AAA, and bilayer AB structures show a net magnetic polarization. In trilayer GQDs, the magnetization is significantly reduced as a result of the increment in electric field intensity, independently of the size. In contrast, for bilayer AB structures, total spin moment is only very slightly affected by the applied field.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Stacked graphene quantum dots
Electric field
Structural properties
Electronic properties
Magnetic properties
DFT

Centro para el Desarrollo de la Nanociencia y la Nanotecnología

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: DL acknowledges partial financial support from Centers of Excellence with BASAL/ANID financing, AFB180001 , CEDENNA. MEMR acknowledges support from Mexican CONACYT under Grant CB 2017-2018/A1-S-8218 .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para:
a) Viewed from above, the common picture of a AA- and AAA-stacked triangular graphene quantum dots with zigzag edge shape. b) A lateral view of the AAA-Stacked system, highlighting the relevant interatomic and interlayer distances. c) Top view of stacked bilayer quantum dot with AB alignment. In all cases the schematic representation corresponds to a structure with side length equal to 12 a
cc .

Distance between monolayers in the investigated bi-layer structures. (a, c, e) correspond to AA- and (b, d, f) to AB-configuration. a)–b) Distance between flakes (like d 1 in Fig. 1b). c) and d) are like a 1, e) and f) are like a 2 in Fig. 1b, respectively. Line colors correspond to GQDs sizes 10–16.
Distances between layers in tri-flake structures of AAA- and ABA-configuration (see blue prints in Fig. 1b, for reference): a) d 1 b) d 2, and c) is d 3. Line colors correspond to sizes 10–16.

As in Fig. 2; calculated layer “widths” but in the cases of AAA and ABA triple graphene quantum dots (see text and vertical blue arrows depicted in Fig. 1b for references). a) a 1 b) is a 2 and c) is a 3. The line color corresponds indicates dot size, 10–16 a
cc , respectively.

Effect of applied electric field - varying from 0.0 to 1.0 V/Å - on spin-polarized energy spectra of double and triple triangular graphene quantum dots (for ease of understanding, the electric field scale is given below.). AA-, AB-, AAA- and ABA-configurations are arranged from top to bottom. Columns correspond to the sizes of GQDs flakes. The line color indicates spin-up (black) and spin-down (red) polarization. In all cases, zero energy is chosen at the Fermi level.

Energy spectrum in double and triple triangular graphene quantum dots. Left panel: variation with respect to dot size from 10 to 16 a
cc . Right panel: variation with respect to the electric field from 0.0 to 1.0 V/Å. For ease of understanding, scales are shown below. Line color indicates spin-up (black) and spin-down (red) polarization. In all cases, zero energy is chosen at the Fermi level.

head.ce:title: Electronic and magnetic properties of stacked graphene quantum dots

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The electronic properties of vertically coupled stacked graphene quantum dots (GQDs) of triangular shape are investigated using density functional theory, including the influence of applied electric field. Both bilayer and trilayer configurations with different sizes are considered, and quantum dot edges are assumed to be passivated with hydrogen atoms. The electric field has, indeed, an effect on the relative positions of atoms in the layers and also on the inter-layer distances, although it is very slight, just reaching up to half an Angstrom. Electronic states in the dots are not largely affected by the electric field in the case of AA and AAA stacks, but significant variations are induced by it in the case of AB and ABA structures, mainly in the states with energies in the vicinity if the Fermi level. Magnetic features are reported for zigzag structures via the calculation of total spin moment. No magnetic response associated with spin is present on the case of AA bilayer GQDs, whereas zigzag-edged trilayer ABA and AAA, and bilayer AB structures show a net magnetic polarization. In trilayer GQDs, the magnetization is significantly reduced as a result of the increment in electric field intensity, independently of the size. In contrast, for bilayer AB structures, total spin moment is only very slightly affected by the applied field.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: In this work we investigate the features of the electronic states in graphene-based systems consisting of vertically coupled triangular quantum dots. As known, graphene, a carbon two-dimensional (2D) structure with atoms arranged in a lattice of hexagonal symmetry, has shown remarkable optical, transport and electronic properties [1–3]. GQDs are 2D fragments sizing below 100 nm. They have found a large number of useful applications in the most diverse fields. It can be mentioned, for instance, optoelectronics, sensor fabrication, energy conversion (including photovoltaics) and storage, bio-imaging, catalysis and electro-chemistry. A number of articles reviewing these and other GQD practical uses have been published within the last decade [4–20]. Physical and chemical properties of GQDs are suitably modified by changing their geometry or by applying electromagnetic fields. Also, edge functionalization or the inclusion of impurities can affect those features in a suitable way [21–27]. The changes of size and shape in GQDs can induce the modulation of their band-gap and optical response [21,26,27]. Triangular, hexagonal, and diamond shaped structures have been reported [28–32]. Particularly, the work by Lu et al. highlighted the possibility of practical achievement for GQDs of different shapes, with the transition towards a triangular one after appropriate thermal treatment [33]. Moreover, it is shown that the particular dot geometry also has to do with its electronic features [34].

Many theoretical investigations dealing with physico-chemical properties of GQDs have been performed [23,26,29–32,34–47]. Modifying the size of the GQD implies a change in the energy band gap. In general, this quantity diminishes when the amount of atoms in the GQD increases, independently of the dot geometry [26].

Stacked graphene-based complexes have also been studied. The layer-by-layer AB-stacked bilayer graphene growth was reported in Ref. [48], whereas selective fabrication of free-standing ABA and ABC trilayer graphene is reported in [49]. Fabricated dual-gated Bernal-stacked bilayer graphene devices demonstrate remarkable fine control over valley isospin degrees of freedom using a perpendicular electric field [50]. Among theoretical investigations, the properties of plasmon-polaritons hosted by AA and AB-stacked bilayer graphene are discussed in [51]. Recently, half-metallic behavior of Bernal stacked graphene multilayers was theoretically investigated [52]. With regard to GQDs, Eich et al. have produced coupled active quantum dots in bilayer graphene [53], while Fu and co-workers fabricated multilayered GQD environments and reported about their optical and electric features. On the other hand, manipulation of the electronic properties of stacked graphene structures via layer twisting shows that the opto-electronic properties could be modulated by changing the amplitude of the relative rotation angle [54–62].

With regard to magnetic response of QDs and other graphene-based structures, density functional theory (DFT), tight-binding, and mean-field studies in systems with different shapes have been conducted in recent times, including the influence defects, size, and electric and/or magnetic field effects [29,30,43,44,46,47,63–66]. Non-magnetic (NM), ferromagnetic (FM) and antiferromagnetic (AFM) states, with their associated optical responses, are discussed. Magnetic and thermodynamic behaviors of graphene-like QDs, studied under Monte Carlo simulation with ferrimagnetic mixed-spin Ising model appears reported in Ref. [66]. The study of magnetic properties in AA-bilayer GQDs via mean-field tight-binding and density functional theory (DFT) has shown a total AFM behavior of the ground state [67]. In addition, bilayer triangular graphene flakes investigated with DFT become AFM due to pairing between edge states [47].

Ab initio DFT-based calculation approaches that take into account spin polarization constitute a suitable tool for investigating the magnetic features of two-dimensional heterostructures. In particular, they would provide for a more accurate description of Van der Waals (VdW) interactions between stacked monolayers. In accordance, this work points at investigating the structural and electronic properties of vertically coupled GQDs of triangular shape (flakes), including the influence of applied electric field, using DFT. Both bilayer and trilayer configurations with different sizes are considered, and quantum dot edges are assumed to be passivated with hydrogen atoms. Then, magnetic features are reported for zigzag structures via the calculation of total spin moment. The article continues in the following order: Section 2 outlines the theoretical framework of the study. In Section 3 we aim at presenting and discussing the obtained results. Finally, Section 4 contains the corresponding conclusions.

Systems under consideration are shown in Fig. 1 . That is, stacked -equilateral- triangular bilayer and trilayer GQDs. In the study, lateral size of the triangle, L, ranks from 10 to 16 times the distance between nearest carbon atoms, a
cc , in the monolayer. Electric field, when applied, is assumed to be directed along the vertical -stacking- direction. In each case, the calculation of electronic states was performed via ab initio spin-resolved DFT with the use of SIESTA package [68], within the Atomic Simulation Environment [69]. The numerical process, employs implemented norm-conserving pseudopotentials; with the basis set consisting of double-ζ, single polarized, localized atomic orbitals (the reader may find more details in the corresponding SIESTA tutorials at https://docs.siesta-project.org/projects/siesta/tutorials/). Structural relaxation is achieved through conjugate gradient minimization, ensuring forces below 0.04 eV/Å on each atom. Different approaches to exchange-correlation interaction were considered for the sake of comparison: local density approximation (LDA), generalized gradient approximations (GGAs) and, finally, VdW interaction was incorporated. Several approaches to include the VdW interaction are developed. It is possible to mention the empirical dispersion in the form of the so-called DFT-D3 approximation (Kohn-Sham DFT with three-body nonadditivity terms) [70], as well as several self-consistent approximations through different exchange-correlation functionals [71–73]. In this work, we select the optB88-VdW (VdW-KBM) exchange-correlation functional, with the use of Dion-Rydberg-Schröder-Langreth-Lundqvist (DRSLL) parameterization [71]. This choice ensures a suitable inclusion of nonlocal charge fluctuations, which are underestimated by the GGA and overestimated by LDA functional. Moreover, VdW-KBM yields a good description of the adsorption energies as well as an adequate one for the geometry. Total magnetic moment of each investigated structure is determined by collecting the corresponding spin contributions, within SIESTA as well.

The configurations analyzed in this work include vertically coupled bilayer AA, AB, and trilayer AAA, ABA quantum dot stacks. As can be noticed from Fig. 1, the first two display piled structures with atoms of the upper monolayers placed exactly above the corresponding ones in the lower layers. Meanwhile, the ABA stacking has the intermediate triangular layer laterally shifted -in Bernal configuration- by a distance of 1 a
cc with respect to the bottom and top ones, in such a way that atoms of it are positioned directly at the center of carbon atom hexagons belonging to outer graphene flakes. Then, bilayer AB stack would come out from the removal of upper A-aligned triangular quantum dot. Initially, both zigzag and armchair edge configurations were considered. After the preparation, it was shown that only zigzag-edged GQDs exhibit total spin moment different from zero, so further research was conducted only for this type of triangle side.

At an initial stage of the work, as shown in [74], we investigated a single-layer triangular graphene flake, varying its size from 5 to 50 a
cc , with zigzag and armchair edges. It was shown that only GQDs with zigzag exhibit values of spin moment different from zero, so further research has been carried out just for this specific edge geometry. The chosen sizes 10–16 a
cc correspond to having 5-6-7-8 hexagons at the base of triangle. The choice of such sizes was primarily due to the fact that, on the one hand, such flakes were big enough as to quantize electron energy, and exhibit -as will be shown below- different configurations of HOMO-LUMO levels (from chemical terms HOMO: highest occupied molecular orbital -equivalent to valence band states-; and LUMO: lowest unoccupied molecular orbital -equivalent to conduction band states). On the other hand, they are not so large and the electronic discrete spectrum remains with good visual resolution.

Let us first analyze some structural features under the influence of an external electric field. Initially, all atoms in each flake localized on the same plane, but after relaxation of the structure, their relative position changed, which is clearly observed from Fig. 1b, where some kind of structural bending of the triple GDD can be noticed. We use the following notation: d 1 and d 2 are the distances between nearest points of neighboring flakes, and d 3 is the distance between two outer flakes. Meanwhile, a 1-a 2-a 3 are the differences between maximum and minimum distances of atoms in the lateral plane of the flake; i.e., what could be understood as a kind of flake “thickness”.

Analyzing the effect of electric field on the curvature of flakes and the distance between them, in Fig. 2a you can see a relative insensitivity to the electric field less than 0.5 V/Å, but with a further field strength increment the distance it begins to increase. At the same time it is not possible to distinguish regularity with the sizes. This will also be seen below when considering the energy structure for a two-flake AA configuration, where the spectrum begins to change significantly only when the electric field values are greater than 0.5 V/Å. Fig. 2c–f shows how the mutual position of atoms in one plane changes. Apparently, the largest (three times) such a difference is for the AB configuration, for the case of the first flake which was not displaced. It is also possible to notice that, for AA coupled QDs, differences a 1 and a 2 exhibit distinct monotonic behaviors for sufficiently high field intensity. In fact, a 1 grows and a 2 diminishes. But for the AB two-flakes, a 1 starts increasing quite significantly above 0.5 V/Å while a 2 has a slight oscillatory variation but a strong increment above 0.9 V/Å. This indicates that the interaction between monolayer QDs in the two different two-flake configurations has particular qualitative and quantitative influence on the electric field effect over in-flake atomic distances. With regard to the inter-flake relative disposition, d 1, the general trend in both cases is to augment with the field. That is, a strong enough electric field pushes away the two monolayers. However, oscillations in this quantity are, again, noticed for the AB-configuration and, in some cases, they imply noticeable reductions for field intensities within the range from 0.3 V/Å to 0.6 V/Å.

The determined changes of intra- and inter-layer distances versus the applied electric field strength for triple GQDs are plotted in Figs. 3 and 4
, respectively. It can be concluded that, in general, as noticed from Fig. 3, inter-layer distances, d 1, d 2, and d 3, are growing functions of the field, which is consistent with the above discussed case of bilayer GQDs. So to say, the applied electric field competes with the VdW attraction and, when high enough, causes the further separation between the involved graphene monolayers. What is somehow different in the triple configuration is that individual layer “thickness” show different trends as a consequence of the increment in applied field intensity. So, while a 2 undergoes a steady growth in both AAA and ABA setups, the differences a 1, and a 3, associated to the outermost layers exhibit mixed variations. That is, in triple AAA GQDs these two quantities are rather insensitive to the field until it reaches values above 0.6 V/Å, for which they begin to decrease. But in the case of ABA GQD one may notice slight increasing trends for a 1 along most of the range of field strengths considered, with a stabilization or a weak decreasing for highest field values, depending on dot size; at the time that a 3 shows practically no variation and only above 0.6 V/Å it has a shy diminishing variation. All this may indicate that, somehow, the intermediate layer serves as a kind of screen for the influence of external field on the atomic positioning in the upper layer.

Fig. 5 shows the calculated energy spectra of two- and three-flake GQDs. The columns correspond to monolayer dot sizes from 10 to 16 a
cc and the rows correspond to AA, AB, AAA and ABA configurations. Black and red colors represent spin up and down polarization, respectively. On the horizontal axis, the varying quantity is the electric field strength, going from zero to 1.0 V/Å. In all cases, the energy origin is chosen to be located at the Fermi level value.

In general, there are differences with regard to the way in which the energy spectrum changes as long as an increasing electric field is applied. Besides the influence of QD size, these differences have to do both with the composition and with the number of flakes involved. It can be clearly noticed that the set of allowed energy levels in A-based structures becomes less affected by the presence of electric field, compared to AB-based ones. In fact, independently of dot size, the spectrum of AA GQDs is unaffected by the external probe, whereas changes can be noticed in the AAA case mostly for the highest values of field strength considered. It can also be observed that there is no level splitting due to spin polarization in the AA GQD, thus bearing out the non-magnetic character of this particular double structure. But for triple AAA system, the set of energy levels already shows a spin-related splitting for spin-up and spin-down polarizations. On the other hand, AB-based structures do show a noticeable influence of the electric field, with a lowering of spin-down contributions and the raising of spin-up ones, with a clear spin-related polarization and a clear formation of energy gaps above and below the split set of levels centered at the origin. It is worth mentioning that such a distribution of levels associates with the presence of edge states, as it was discussed by us for the situation of asymmetric dot staking due to relative twisting [74]. For clearer illustration, Fig. 6 contains a zoom of spectra in the mentioned region. There, it is easier to see that, for each value of the quantity varying on the horizontal axis, there is a pair of columns, one of each depicting energy levels with a single spin polarization. As expected, the increase in dot size produces a denser level spectrum within the same energy range as is observed from the left panel. It corresponds to the zero applied field case and different values of triangle side. Strong qualitative differences appear when comparing A-based and AB-based structures. For the latter, as long as dot dimensions grow, spin-polarized levels on both sides of the Fermi level tend to cluster rather tightly forming what could be called as quasi-bands. However, spectra of AA and AAA GQDs are sparse sets of levels, indicating a basic distinction between the two kinds of stacking considered.

Asymmetry, related to the configuration of AB double GQD as well as to the odd number of layers in the ABA and AAA triple GQDs, is responsible for a net total magnetic polarization of the nanostructures under consideration. As commented, we are interested in finding out the effect on their magnetic response of applying an external static electric field. In accordance, Fig. 7 contains the calculated total magnetic moment (in units of Bohr magneton, μ
B ) for the three cases that show nonzero magnetic polarization. Clearly, higher values of such a quantity appear for the larger QD sizes, and this has to do with the greater number of edge states with unpaired spins involved. From the quantitative point of view, configurations including displaced B-layers lead to the a higher degree of spin impairment for edge states. As can be observed, the configuration that becomes less sensitive to field application is AAA, for which the response is more stable thanks to the symmetric geometry. In this sense, one can only observe a slight undulation in the curve when field intensity is around 0.7 V/Å, which is more pronounced for larger dots and almost unnoticed for the smallest sizes investigated. On the contrary, total magnetic moment in AB and ABA GQDS undergoes a rather strong reduction (nearly, as much as three times) when field intensity changes throughout the interval of values considered. This indicates that there is an intense spin interaction for edge states whose energies are in the vicinity of Fermi level. One just has to look at the right panel in Fig. 6 and notice that, for those configurations, there are many pairs of spin-up and spin-down states for which the influence of the applied field adjusts very close energy values.

In this work, we have investigated the influence of an externally applied static electric field on the structural, electronic and magnetic properties of vertically stacked triangular graphene quantum dots in both the bilayer and trilayer configurations, considering different values for the dot size. This study was performed via first-principles calculation using density functional theory with Van der Waals exchange-correlation functionals as implemented in the SIESTA code.

We have determined slight changes in the relevant distances between atoms in each layer, and between different layers due to the application of an electric field. Moreover, the spectrum of energy levels also shows variations due to the influence of such external probe, with the exception of AA double dots. This effect is more pronounced for the AB and ABA nanostructures, in whose cases the edge-like states arranged around the Fermi level are significantly displaced depending on their particular spin polarization (up or down). Then, this fact is reflected in the magnetic response of the systems. We have verified that AA triangular graphene quantum dots are of non-magnetic character, while AAA ones have small values of their total magnetic moment, that is practically unaffected by the applied field. However, AB and ABA stacks are clearly magnetic systems, with a significant dependence on the dot size of the total magnetic moment which actually undergoes strong reduction as long as the electric field intensity becomes higher.

The present work has helped to identify some features of the magnetic response of bi- and tri-layer triangular zigzag graphene quantum dots under electric fields which could be of interest when applications of these structures are sought for. A logical continuation of this research is the investigation of relative layer twisting, in order to detect some particular manifestation of electronic (such as energy gap) and magnetic (total spin-related moment) properties at given rotation angles. This will be performed for both bi- and tri-layer systems, with the latter promising to be interesting enough due to the different twisting combinations possible. Work along these lines is already in progress and will be published elsewhere.

AT performed numerical calculations and prepared figs. DL processed results. JDC wrote codes, processed results. MEMR processed results and wrote manuscript. All authors participated in conceptualization and reviewed the manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare the following financial interests/personal relationships which may be considered as potential competing interests: Miguel Eduardo Mora-Ramos reports financial support was provided by National Council of Science and Technology, Mexico. David Laroze reports financial support was provided by University of Santiago de Chile Center for Nanoscience and Nanotechnology Development.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: DL acknowledges partial financial support from Centers of Excellence with BASAL/ANID financing, AFB180001, CEDENNA. MEMR acknowledges support from Mexican CONACYT under Grant CB 2017-2018/A1-S-8218.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Graphene quantum dots: synthesis and applications
